The Anatomy of a Note

Pitch is a speed, loudness is a size, timbre is a recipe — and one vibrating string explains all three. Every figure on this page makes real sound. Turn your volume to something kind.

Scroll down to read ↓

This series is about music theory — but it refuses to start where the textbooks start, with staff lines and Italian vocabulary. Music theory is downstream of physics. Scales, chords, and keys all trace back in part to vibrating objects and the ear that evolved to hear them. So we will start with sound itself and introduce theory from there.

The wave at the top of this page is a musical note — the A below middle C. Tap it and the line becomes your actual speaker output, captured and redrawn a hundred times a second. This post explains the main features of that line and how they affect what you hear.

Step 1 · Pitch is a speedThe note under the microscope

A sound is a pressure ripple in air. A musical sound — a note — is a ripple that repeats. Something pushed the air, and pushed it again, and again, on a schedule. The single most important number in all of music is just how fast that schedule runs: 

frequency = repeats per second        (measured in hertz, Hz)

110 Hz  →  the A on a guitar's fifth string
220 Hz  →  the A one octave up
440 Hz  →  the A orchestras tune to

That repeat rate determines pitch. A note isn't a thing, it's a rate — and everything your ear calls "higher" or "lower" is your brain reporting a rate change.

The instrument below is an oscilloscope you can play. Press play and drag the frequency slider: the pitch climbs, and the repeating shape on screen physically squeezes — more repeats packed into the same slice of time. The orange ticks bracket exactly one repeat. Then try the shape buttons: the pitch stays put, but the character of the sound changes — buzzy, hollow, reedy. Hold that thought; it's Step 3.

Aside · 1,000 years of not knowing

Musicians tuned by ear for millennia without knowing what pitch was. The frequency–pitch connection wasn't nailed down until the 17th century, when Galileo (and, separately, the French friar Marin Mersenne) connected vibration counts to perceived pitch. Mersenne measured frequency with no electronics at all: he used ropes tens of meters long, slow enough to count the swings by eye, then scaled up by the laws he'd found. The numbers in this article are all descendants of that trick.

Two details before we move on. First: your ear hears ratios, not differences. The hop from 110 to 220 Hz sounds the same size as the hop from 220 to 440 — each is a doubling. That logarithmic fact will quietly run the entire series. Second: the slider stops at 880 Hz, but your ear runs from about 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz — ten doublings, twenty times the range of human vision (one octave of light, if you're generous). Music lives mostly in the bottom third.

Step 2 · One string, all of itPluck

Now for the machine that makes notes — the oldest one we have. A stretched string, fixed at both ends, obeys a single rule: every point accelerates toward the average of its neighbours. That's the entire physics. Written as code, it's three lines: 

// the wave equation, discretized: each point chases its neighbours' average
for (let i = 1; i < N - 1; i++) {
  v[i] += k * (y[i-1] - 2*y[i] + y[i+1]);   // curvature pulls
  y[i] += v[i];                              // motion follows
}

The string below runs exactly that loop. Grab it, pull, and let go. (It's drawn 80× slower than it sounds — a real 110 Hz string is a blur.) Watch what happens after release: the kink you made splits in two, the halves run to the ends, bounce, and cross — and out of that traffic emerges a stable, repeating wobble. The repetition rate of that wobble is the string's frequency. The fixed ends select stable repeating modes from the wave equation.

Now watch the coloured bars under the string, and try the two pluck buttons. Pluck the middle: only a few bars light, and the tone is round, almost flute-like. Pluck near the bridge: the bars light all the way up, and the same string at the same pitch turns thin and twangy. The bars show the components responsible for that difference.

Deep dive: where the sound in this demo actually comes from

The audio isn't a recording of the simulation — at real speed the sim would need ~44,000 steps a second. Instead, the demo uses a key property of the wave equation: every possible motion of the string is a sum of standing waves — shapes sin(πx), sin(2πx), sin(3πx)… that don't travel, just oscillate in place, each at its own frequency f, 2f, 3f…

At the moment you release, the demo projects the string's shape onto the first ten standing waves (a tiny Fourier transform), and hands each one a sine oscillator at n×f with that amplitude, decaying faster for higher n — which is how physical strings behave. The visual is the wave equation; the audio is its Fourier dual. They are the same object in two coordinate systems.

Aside · The first music theory result in history

That frequencies come in the ladder f, 2f, 3f… was known, in geometric disguise, to the Pythagoreans around 500 BC: halve a string and it sounds an octave up; third it and you get the fifth above that. Legend says Pythagoras discovered this listening to blacksmiths' hammers — almost certainly false (hammer pitch doesn't work that way), but the string experiments were real, and they made pitch the first physical quantity ever described by ratios of whole numbers. Two and a half millennia later, this ladder still explains most of what this series will cover.

Step 3 · Timbre is a recipeThe harmonic series

Here is the claim those bars were making, stated plainly. A string can't choose between its standing waves — it does all of them at once. A real plucked note at 110 Hz also contains quieter vibrations at 220, 330, 440, 550 Hz… This ladder is called the harmonic series, the overtones are partials, and the mix — how much of each — is what your ear calls timbre. Same pitch, different recipe: that's why a trumpet isn't a flute.

The same idea runs in both directions. In 1822 Joseph Fourier showed that any repeating wave — including the square and saw shapes from the oscilloscope — can be built from pure sine waves at harmonic frequencies. The mixing board below is that theorem made playable: eight partials of C3, eight faders. The faint coloured lines are the ingredients; the bright line is their sum, and the sound.

Things worth trying. Pull everything down except h1: a pure sine, the only sound with no recipe — soft, anonymous, the vanilla of sound. Add odd harmonics at 1/n (the clarinet preset) and the wave squares off — a clarinet really does suppress its even partials; that's the physics of a tube closed at one end. Slide in everything at 1/n and you approach the sawtooth — the brass-like buzz the oscilloscope made. And notice what you can't do: no fader setting changes the pitch. The recipe owns the flavour; the fundamental owns the name.

Deep dive: the missing fundamental — pitch is an inference

Strange and important: if you play partials at 220, 330, and 440 Hz with no 110 Hz component at all, you still hear a 110 Hz note. The ear notices the spacing of the ladder and reports the rung that should be there. This is called the missing fundamental, and it's why a phone speaker that physically cannot reproduce a bass guitar's 41 Hz low E still lets you hear the bass line: the upper partials carry the pattern, and your brainstem does the arithmetic.

Pitch, in other words, is not a measurement. It's a perceptual inference about what repetition rate best explains the evidence. Keep that in mind whenever this series says a note "is" some frequency — what it really is, is a pattern your ear has opinions about.

Aside · Helmholtz and the glass spheres

Hermann von Helmholtz confirmed the recipe theory of timbre in the 1850s with no oscilloscope and no Fourier analyzer — just a graded set of hollow glass spheres ("Helmholtz resonators"), each of which sings along with one frequency only. Holding sphere after sphere to his ear while instruments played, he could read off a note's recipe one partial at a time. His 1863 book On the Sensations of Tone founded the science of hearing — and part two of this series steals its central idea.

EpilogueThree properties of a note

Dissection complete. A note is a repeating pressure wave, and your ear cares about exactly three things in it: 

frequency  → pitch    (the repeat rate; heard in ratios, not differences)
amplitude  → loudness (the size of the swing)
spectrum   → timbre   (the recipe of harmonics riding on the fundamental)

Everything we built today concerned one note alone. But music begins — really begins — the moment there are two. Play two notes together and their harmonic ladders overlap: some pairs of rungs line up and fuse, others land close-but-not-quite and beat against one another. That beating is audible as roughness; smoother combinations are called consonant. Part two examines which pairs sound consonant and why.

Continued in Part two: Why Notes Agree — beats, whole-number ratios, and harmonic alignment: consonance as physics you can hear and see.

Part two: Why Notes Agree →

Sound by Tone.js; figures are WebGPU fragment shaders fed by live analyser data. Standing on the shoulders of: Fourier (1822) · Helmholtz, On the Sensations of Tone (1863)
